title: The oldest tool, about to carry new weight
paras: Let's begin with the oldest tool in the box — and the one that's about to carry new weight. Settlement boundaries are the oldest device in the rural policy planner's kit, and the least examined. You draw a line around a town or village. Inside it, development is acceptable in principle. Outside, the land is countryside, and restraint policies apply. Nearly every rural local plan in England relies on the device, under a bewildering variety of names — settlement boundaries, development limits, village envelopes, built-up area boundaries, settlement development limits. And the variety of names is itself the evidence. A tool used everywhere has never acquired a common method.

The lines descend from the post-war containment tradition. And the first thing to understand about the boundaries on today's proposals maps is that most were never drawn under any stated methodology at all. They were converted from older designations, amended incrementally without recorded criteria, and defended at examination on assertion. Doncaster's 2018 methodology paper is candid about this: its development limits were inferred from the policy areas of a 1998 U-D-P, complete with documented anomalies. North Yorkshire still publishes its abolished predecessor districts' boundaries as separate legacy datasets — each drawn under a different, vanished plan. The consequences are familiar at appeal. Boundaries that cut through gardens. Boundaries that exclude infill plots for no articulable reason. Boundaries that include land nobody can now explain.

For forty years the stakes were high, but familiar. The boundary decided which side of the countryside policies a site fell, and the weight of the line rose and fell with the plan's health. Where the plan was up to date and delivering, the boundary held. Where the five-year supply failed, or the plan aged, the tilted balance swept the line aside — and a generation of appeal decisions turned on whether restrictive settlement policies were out of date. The boundary has always been a policy line whose force was conditional.

The December 2025 draft Framework restructures the mechanism. It requires local plans to identify a clear spatial strategy, setting out settlement boundaries — or criteria — that determine where the presumption in favour of sustainable development applies. Within settlements, proposals are supported unless the harms substantially outweigh the benefits. Outside them, the presumption applies only to a defined list of development types, including circumstances of under-delivery and certain rural categories. National decision-making policies, against which inconsistent local policies carry very limited weight, and a thirty-month plan cycle — with the old system closing to submissions at the end of 2026 — complete the picture. If the final Framework holds this structure, the boundary stops being a soft policy line and becomes the switch that turns the presumption on or off. And lines of the pedigree I've just described are about to carry presumption-switching weight. Promoters will test the weak ones, site by site. Objectors will test the generous ones. And inspectors will test the methodology — where one exists.

title: The legal frame: Braintree and the limits of the line
paras: Now to the legal frame — Braintree, and the limits of the line. The courts have set the frame, and it's a humbling one for anyone who regards the line on the proposals map as self-executing. In Braintree District Council against the Secretary of State for Communities and Local Government, reported at 2018, E-W-C-A Civ 610, the Court of Appeal held that the Framework contains no definition of a settlement or a village, prescribes no minimum number of dwellings, and does not require that a settlement be defined by a boundary fixed in an adopted or emerging plan. Whether a group of dwellings constitutes a settlement is a matter of fact and planning judgment for the decision-maker. And isolation carries its ordinary meaning — physical separateness from other built form.

Two consequences flow from that. A boundary can't conclusively define what is or isn't a settlement for national policy purposes. And land outside the line isn't automatically isolated countryside. A dwelling beyond the boundary may still be at a settlement as a matter of fact, and the isolated-homes policy isn't engaged merely because the proposals map says so. The boundary is a policy tool whose force depends entirely on the quality of the plan that adopts it — and on the plan's continuing health.

The plan-making translation of the same principle is the distinction the only published guide to the subject insists on. A settlement boundary is not a tool for identifying what is part of a village. It identifies a single cohesive built-up area. And outlying dwellings that are socially part of a settlement — the farmstead that has always belonged to the village, the cottages up the lane — may properly fall outside its boundary. Social and historic membership is one question. Physical cohesion is another. The line answers only the second.

And the corollary runs right through the appeal record of the last decade: the boundary is only ever as strong as the plan's delivery performance. Where housing land supply failed, boundaries were swept aside by the tilted balance. And although the emerging Framework restructures the mechanism, its draft continues to apply the presumption outside settlements where supply or delivery fail. The line does not defend the plan. The plan defends the line.

title: The common core: criteria that recur
paras: So what is the common core — the criteria that recur? Strip away the terminology, and the independently written methodologies converge on a recognisable core of six elements. Start with the built-form principle. The boundary defines the built extent of the settlement, and land is included where it forms part of that built character. The recurring formulation is that dwellings and their curtilages are included where the curtilage closely relates to the character of the built form and is enclosed — with community facilities, their curtilages, and employment sites integral to the settlement added to the standard inclusion list.

Next, the character exclusions: land is excluded where its inclusion would harm the character, structure or form of the settlement. Winchester's formulation is the most quoted — large gardens, paddocks and orchards are excluded on precisely that test — with open spaces at the settlement edge, sports fields and allotments added elsewhere. And there's a consolidated exclusion list that reads as the emerging consensus. Isolated development separated from the main settlement. Agricultural buildings and farmsteads on the fringe. Large gardens and paddocks extending into open countryside. Open recreational facilities with limited built form. Designated green gaps and local green spaces. And buildings whose use is more closely associated with the countryside than the settlement.

The edge cases are resolved by relationship, not proximity — and the South Downs National Park Authority contributes the most refined criterion in the sample. Converted agricultural buildings are included only where they're not visually or physically detached from the settlement. And they're excluded, even when physically adjacent, if they retain a separate character — a separate access, say, or continuing membership of a larger agricultural complex. The principle generalises, and it has an obvious resonance for the growing stock of Class Q conversions at settlement edges, whose boundary status will be contested parcel by parcel. Alongside it sits the physical features rule: boundaries follow, but do not include, clearly defined features on the ground — walls, hedgerows, roads, tracks, watercourses, field boundaries. Its purpose is durability. A line that follows a hedge survives; a line across an open field invites dispute.

Two disciplines complete the core. The first is the commitments cut-off. Reviews are undertaken against a fixed snapshot date, ignoring permissions granted mid-review — with the refinement that unimplemented permissions are excluded, because they're not yet built form, while commenced permissions are included, because their layout is certain. And stale allocations judged unlikely to be built are stripped out on review. The second is the audit trail. The methodologies that read as examination-ready share a format — a numbered criteria set, followed by a settlement-by-settlement schedule in which every boundary change is recorded against the criterion that justifies it, with segment-level scoring sheets operationalising the same idea, stretch by stretch.

title: The five design choices
paras: Now for the five design choices. Beyond the core, the evidence reveals five decisions on which authorities have taken opposite, reasoned positions — the choices a methodology must confront rather than inherit. First: boundaries or criteria? Cornwall abolished settlement boundaries in 2016 and replaced them with criteria — infill of small gaps in otherwise continuous built frontage, rounding off, and previously developed land within or immediately adjoining a settlement at an appropriate scale, with express protection for significant gaps. Nine years of practice record the gains — flexibility, and organic growth across settlements of all sizes — and the cost: persistent interpretive uncertainty over what constitutes a small gap or rounding off, contested application by application. Under the draft Framework, the choice acquires formal status, because boundaries and criteria are alternative mechanisms for defining where the presumption applies. Criteria trade certainty for flexibility, and they relocate the argument from the plan examination into every application. That trade must now be made knowingly.

Second: tight or loose? East Devon documents the deliberate shift — existing boundaries drawn very tightly, expressly to constrain outward growth; new boundaries drawn loosely, to yield a supply of small sites below one hectare, in support of national small-sites policy. Tightness is not a technical property of a boundary. It's a growth-strategy decision. And the neighbourhood-tier warning cuts the same way: a boundary that constrains housing land against strategic policy risks failing the basic conditions altogether. Third: a third way for small villages. Cheshire East defines infill boundaries for smaller villages — a lighter-touch line, admitting modest development without conferring full within-settlement status. North Somerset couples boundaries with a policy admitting schemes of fewer than twenty-five dwellings adjoining the boundary at its service villages. Under a presumption-switching Framework, both devices become ways of calibrating how much presumption a small village receives.

Fourth: district or parish? The spectrum here is wider than commonly appreciated. Districts such as Wiltshire and North Norfolk draw and review the boundaries themselves, with neighbourhood plans permitted to adjust through a sufficiently advanced process. The South Downs National Park Authority operates an explicit deference rule — where a neighbourhood plan designates boundaries, the authority will not review them — and it offers its methodology to parishes as a model. Cornwall expressly delegates the definition of infill settlements and their boundaries to neighbourhood plans. And at the far end stands South Oxfordshire, whose adopted local plan defines no settlement boundaries at all — the entire exercise is delegated to its parishes, supported since January 2026 by the only free-standing published guide to the subject. Full delegation buys local knowledge and ownership. The price is patchwork coverage, inconsistent criteria between adjacent parishes, and the cross-parish geometry, where a boundary crossing a parish line can only be amended on one side. And under the new Framework, the risk sharpens — because unparished or plan-less areas of a fully delegating district may lack the very boundaries or criteria on which the presumption's operation depends.

Fifth: the Green Belt line. Where settlements lie in the Green Belt, the washed-over or inset decision is a distinct assessment — villages judged on their open character and its contribution to Green Belt openness, with a recommendation on insetting each one. Where the settlement boundary is also the Green Belt boundary, altering one alters the other, and that engages the exceptional-circumstances regime for Green Belt change. And the decision-taking corollary, from made neighbourhood plans, is that a boundary policy can provide that proposals outside the line are assessed against Green Belt policy — the line simultaneously fixing the reach of two different national regimes. Boundaries in Green Belt districts carry double freight, and they need double care.

title: The ten-step method and the model criteria
paras: Which brings us to the ten-step method, and the model criteria. The boundary question is logically second; the first is the settlement hierarchy. The fully worked model screened around five hundred and fifty named settlements against a size threshold — one hundred people, or fifty dwellings — then scored the survivors on services, facilities, broadband, employment and public transport, with rural multi-function premises expressly weighted for their community-hub role. And it held the approved methodology constant across three published iterations while the data was refreshed — exactly the discipline an examination rewards. The standard consequence follows: upper tiers receive boundaries, the smallest settlements receive none, and development there is governed by countryside and exceptions policies. Or the hierarchy is expressed as a policy matrix that parishes drawing their own lines must respect.

Synthesised, the method has ten steps. One: decide the instrument — boundaries, criteria or hybrid — as an explicit, recorded strategy decision. Two: decide the tier — who draws, who reviews, what deference neighbourhood boundaries receive, and how cross-parish settlements are handled. Three: settle the hierarchy on the thresholded model, fixing the methodology before applying it. Four: declare the strategy of the line — tight or loose, connected to the small-sites and windfall assumptions. Five: adopt the criteria before drawing anything. Six: fix the snapshot date. Seven: draw by desktop and verify on the ground — G-I-S and aerial imagery first, then planning history for every edge site, and site visits before publication. Eight: write the two-limbed policy. Nine: record every line against a criterion in a settlement-by-settlement schedule. And ten: build in the review regime.

Then the model criteria set — every element of which has survived consultation somewhere in England. The inclusion criteria first. I one: buildings forming the cohesive built-up area. I two: residential curtilages closely related to the built form, and enclosed. I three: community facilities and their curtilages, within or attached to the built form. I four: employment sites integral to the settlement. I five: commenced permissions and firm allocations at the snapshot date — with a conscious decision on whether allocation green buffers enter the line. I six: previously developed land within the built-up area — or, where strategy supports it, adjoining it. The exclusion criteria. E one: land whose inclusion would harm the character, structure or form of the settlement, including large gardens, paddocks and orchards. E two: isolated or detached development, however socially connected. E three: agricultural buildings, farmsteads and converted rural buildings on the fringe, unless visually and physically attached and of shared character. E four: open recreational land with limited built form. E five: green gaps, local green spaces and coalescence buffers. E six: buildings whose use is more closely associated with the countryside. E seven: unimplemented permissions and stale allocations. And the drawing rules. D one: follow, but do not include, defined physical features. D two: boundaries need not be continuous — distinct built clusters may carry more than one line, with the gaps expressly protected. D three: identify where the line coincides with a Green Belt boundary, and apply the alteration disciplines. D four: the boundary identifies the cohesive built-up area, not the social or historic settlement. And D five: every segment assessed and recorded against the criteria.

The policy that carries the line is two-limbed. It states what is supported within the boundary — conditioned on character, design codes and the rest of the development plan, never absolute. And it states what governs outside it, tying the outside limb to the countryside, Green Belt or exceptions policies that then apply. A boundary without an outside limb is a line without consequences. The best policies anticipate their own failure modes. The made-plan example expressly withholds support from development within the boundary that would, individually or cumulatively, extend linear form along a road or watercourse, out of keeping with a historic nucleated pattern — a drafted answer to the classic within-boundary ribbon problem. And on display: layered justification of contested segments — current aerial imagery, historic Ordnance Survey mapping, annotated features — with clean cartography, distinct line styles, keys, scale, and cross-reference to the criteria.

title: What survives examination — and what falls
paras: Finally: what survives examination — and what falls. The patterns from the examination and appeal record are consistent. What survives? Boundaries adopted through a published, criteria-based methodology with a settlement-by-settlement audit trail — where an inspector can trace any contested line back to a stated criterion and a recorded judgment, and a challenged segment can be defended with its own layered mapping evidence rather than institutional memory. Boundaries aligned with a thresholded hierarchy inherit the hierarchy's evidence. Criteria drawn from the common core are hard to attack, precisely because they recur independently across the country. Two-limbed policies give decision-makers something to apply. And a boundary policy that expressly connects to the growth strategy — stating why the lines are tight or loose, and how the windfall assumptions depend on it — meets the soundness question of justification head on.

What falls? Inherited lines defended on assertion fall first — where no methodology exists, every anomaly is exposed. Boundaries doing silent policy work fall next. Lines drawn tight to suppress growth, while the plan claims a positive strategy, invite modification — and the record includes an inspector rewriting an over-restrictive countryside policy at examination, to restore the flexibility national policy expects for rural vitality. At the neighbourhood tier, the failure has a sharper edge, because a boundary constraining housing land against the local plan's strategic policies can fail the basic conditions and sink the plan. Loosely drafted criteria relocate the dispute rather than resolve it. One-limbed policies, silent on the outside — or forgetful of the Green Belt regime the line also fixes — unravel in decision-taking. And above all, out-of-date plans. The appeal record of the last decade is dominated by boundaries swept aside where supply failed, and the draft Framework continues to apply the presumption outside settlements where supply or delivery fail.

The inherited-anomaly problem is, at bottom, a failure of review — and the evidence supports a review discipline. A stated cycle of five years, or each plan review. Automatic reassessment when the plan or strategy above changes, and when major committed development completes. And defined triggers for earlier review — housing delivery shortfall, significant demographic or economic change, infrastructure schemes altering a settlement's sustainability, and environmental changes such as revised flood mapping. Equally firmly: boundary change happens only through plan review, never by concession in decision-taking. That is what keeps the line's integrity between reviews.

Let me close with a candid note on the evidence, carried from the source research. The case law is verified. The examination and appeal patterns are drawn from the harvested documents, the made neighbourhood plans and established practice, and they're stated at pattern level. A structured sample of inspector reports and appeal decisions — replacing pattern-level statements with counted outcomes — and the verification pass against the final Framework are the next phases, and this guide will be updated with both. The opportunity, meanwhile, is real. The rural planning system is about to hang the presumption on lines most authorities cannot currently justify. The components of a defensible methodology have all been tested somewhere in public. What has never existed is the synthesis. Authorities that adopt one deliberately, before their lines are tested, hold the high ground. Promoters who understand the methodology know precisely where inherited lines are weakest. And parishes drawing under delegation need the same framework, so the map does not fragment.
